learning_outcomes.description: Know and understand the legislative requirements and obligations of being a psychologist
Have a good knowledge of the APS Code of Ethics and the associated APS Guidelines
Demonstrate ethical analysis and decision-making in relation to clients, colleagues, the profession of psychology and other health professionals
Apply the theoretical principles of the codes of practice to daily situations arising in psychology practice
Understand the key factors to be considered in research design
Identify potential ethical issues in a research context

Recognise and reflect on interprofessional practice skills including role clarification, team functioning, interprofessional communication, conflict management, collaborative leadership, and client-centred care.

assessments.due_date:
31/07/2026 - 7/08/2026

The assessment will be completed in the tutorial class in Week 1 or Week 2, with a final submission date set a week later. The due time at 4pm (which is when the later tutorial ends).
assessments.task_description:
This is a team assessment and all team members are expected to contribute equally to the team’s assessment items during the tutorial. Teams will be assigned prior to the tutorial. There are two (2) parts to this assessment both based upon a client video that your team will choose during the tutorial class.

Part one requires you to complete a worksheet and will draw on the online and face-to-face content of the GEMS IPCP module one, focusing on role-clarification and client-centred care.

Length: There is no word limit for the worksheet.

 

Part two requires you to complete a table and will draw on the on the online and face-to-face content of the GEMS IPCP module one, focusing on interprofessional communication, team functioning and client-centred care.

Length: There is no word limit for the worksheet

Teams will need to submit both assessment pieces in the Assessment tab on the IPCP Learn.UQ course site prior to the end of the tutorial class. Additional information regarding submission will be provided on the IPCP Learn.UQ course site.

Please refer to the assessment information on Learn.UQ for further details and requirements for the assessment.


The IPCP module is a Pass/Fail assessment task.

  • Students who pass the module will receive a course grade based on their passing the remaining must pass assessment/s in the course.
  • Students who are unable to attend the module because they are away from Brisbane or ill on the day of the tutorial class, will be given an opportunity to complete the assessment individually. Successful completion of the assessment will again enable a PASS grade for the module as above depending on other pass requirements within the course.
  • For all students who submit a genuine attempt at the assessment but do not achieve a pass grade for their submitted work, they will be given a second opportunity to pass the module by completing a reflective essay. If this is task is successfully completed, a grade for the course will be awarded based on the remaining assessment in the course.
  • Students who do not engage with the module and do not submit a genuine attempt, will be given an opportunity to complete a 5,000 word assignment on teamwork. Successful completion of this assignment will enable the student to be eligible to pass the course with a maximum grade of 4 (if the remaining assessment in the course would result in a grade higher than 4, a maximum grade of 4 will be awarded).
  • Students who fail the module, who do not complete either the reflective essay or the teamwork assignment above will be awarded a maximum grade of 3 (if the remaining assessment in the course would result in a grade higher than 3, a maximum grade of 3 will be awarded).

Students will be required to hand in five worksheets at the start of class for the last five workshops.


Students will need to submit 4/5 worksheets to pass this section of the course.

assessments.task_description:
Task description:

Much of your professional practice will rely upon the feedback of your peers during group supervision. We want you to consider ethical issues associated with a particular case, present this to your peers and then respond to their feedback. The task will involve the following steps:

1. Form groups: In the first workshop, you will form a group of 4 students from different programs. You will be given an ethical case to respond to. You will need to meet with your group repeatedly to prepare for the presentation.

2. Present your ethical decision-making process: Present to the group a chosen ethical decision-making model and then discuss what you did at each step to arrive at your final decision (about 8 minutes).

3. Discuss your ethical decision-making with the class: The second part of the presentation requires you to facilitate a brief class discussion on the topic (roughly 7 minutes). 


Presentation will involve:

1. Outline case: Provide an overview of the case. 

2. Discuss what guided your response to the dilemma: Describe the ethical theory, principles and legislation that informed your response.

3. Outline your ethical decision making: Describe the stakeholders and what are the alternatives? Describe your ethical decision-making model and the steps you took to arrive at a decision. 

4. Feedback from AI: AI can draw on ethical principles and cases to support decision-making. You will input your response into Copilot and prompt it for a solution using different legislation and ethical principles. You will compare how your response differs to AI.


Expectations regarding group participation and contribution: 

The expectation is that you make an active contribution that is discernible to the other members of the group. The process of preparation for your presentation involves a number of tasks, including (but not limited to): finding an ethical decision-making model; working through the varied steps of the model (e.g. identifying stakeholders; researching codes and legislation; generating pros and cons for options; etc.); contributing to group discussion; preparing the various aspects of the presentation; and delivering various parts of the presentation. It is expected that the tasks will be distributed equitably between group members. 


Instructions:

You will use roughly 4 slides to present your response to the ethical dilemma. 

You will submit these slides prior to your presentation. 

You will submit a Member Contribution and Declaration Form outlining your contribution to the presentation.

You need to have each team member present at least part of the presentation.

The focus of the assessment is depth, thought and analysis: we want to see that you have meaningfully engaged in the ethical decision-making process.

Assessment guidelines will be made available at the start of the course.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Roughly 4 slides and 'Member Contribution and Declaration Form' will be submitted via Turnitin prior to that relevant workshop.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
Outline:

Students will be asked to present a brief research proposal to the class. This will allow each student to discuss their research, highlight any potential difficulties, seek feedback on their proposal, and identify potential opportunities for collaboration. Students will be asked to respond to questions and suggestions. Supervisors are encouraged to attend seminar presentations and students will be responsible for notifying them of the nominated time and date. All students are required to attend the two 2-hour presentations and to present their own research.


Format:

Students will have a maximum of 7 minutes in which to present their research: 5 minutes presentation and 2 minutes for questions. The presentation should name their proposed supervisor on the first slide. The presentation should focus on a brief rationale, the research design and potential challenges. This is an opportunity to refine your research proposals and seek input from peers and supervisors. Penalties will apply for non–adherence to the allocated time. All students who are not presenting will be asked to generate questions and to provide feedback to the presenter. A marking outline for presentations will be give to students early in semester.


Slides:

Prior to their presentation workshop, students will also be asked to submit their presentation slides which will be uploaded.
assessments.submission_guidelines: This assessment is completed in person. The abstract is submitted via Blackboard.

assessments.due_date:
9/11/2026 - 10/11/2026

Students will be assigned a time to complete the role play within the designated dates.
assessments.task_description:
Ethical Clinic:

Individual students will be asked to role play an ethical dilemma in a mock psychology clinic - the "Ethical Clinic". In the Ethical Clinic they will negotiate an ethical dilemma, while interacting with actors playing clients, colleagues and managers.


Students need to demonstrate competency in assessing and responding appropriately to a dilemma during the role play. Students will also provide written responses regarding the Ethical Clinic at the end of the role play, assessing their ethical reasoning.


Students will need to demonstrate the ability to identify the ethical issues, an understanding of the relevant ethical and legal guidelines appropriate to the dilemma, the capacity to respond to the dilemma in an appropriate manner and the ability to self-reflect on their own performance. The actual assessment will run for about 30 minutes, but students may be required to wait until it is their turn to be assessed.
assessments.submission_guidelines: This assessment is completed in person. The written component of the assessment will be completed immediately after the role play.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

learning_activities.topic:
GEMS Interprofessional Collaborative Practice Module

GEMS Interprofessional Collaborative Practice Module (Friday 25th July 2025 or Friday 1st August 2025)

You will work in small teams in the tutorial with students from: Audiology, Dentistry, Dietetics, Nursing, Occupational Therapy, Physiotherapy, Psychology, Social Work and Speech Pathology. There is one module consisting of on-line preparation and a face-to-face tutorial class. You will work in your small team to engage in a range of learning activities to develop your knowledge and skills to help prepare you for working in interprofessional collaborative teams when caring for clients. 


Learning outcomes: L08
learning_activities.topic_description: GEMS Interprofessional Collaborative Practice Module GEMS Interprofessional Collaborative Practice Module (Friday 25th July 2025 or Friday 1st August 2025) You will work in small teams in the tutorial with students from: Audiology, Dentistry, Dietetics, Nursing, Occupational Therapy, Physiotherapy, Psychology, Social Work and Speech Pathology. There is one module consisting of on-line preparation and a face-to-face tutorial class. You will work in your small team to engage in a range of learning activities to develop your knowledge and skills to help prepare you for working in interprofessional collaborative teams when caring for clients.

learning_activities.topic:
Ethical Foundations & Professional Regulation

During your psychology training, most courses teach specific skills to treat your clients. By contrast, ethics training influences every aspect of your clinical practice and your every interaction.

This initial workshop will provide an overview for the course. We will discuss ethical theory, particularly utilitarian vs. principal-based ethics. We will discuss and consider the Code of Conduct. We will discuss ethical decision-making frameworks and the role of AHPRA in regulating professional psychology practice.


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L08
learning_activities.topic_description: Ethical Foundations & Professional Regulation During your psychology training, most courses teach specific skills to treat your clients. By contrast, ethics training influences every aspect of your clinical practice and your every interaction. This initial workshop will provide an overview for the course. We will discuss ethical theory, particularly utilitarian vs. principal-based ethics. We will discuss and consider the Code of Conduct. We will discuss ethical decision-making frameworks and the role of AHPRA in regulating professional psychology practice.

learning_activities.topic:
Professional Ethics I & Research Ethics

We will discuss professional issues around establishing appropriate boundaries during psychology practice. We will discuss the importance of maintaining confidentiality.

We will consider ethical issues related to conducting research. We will also consider different research methodologies appropriate for your masters thesis. We will start to workshop ideas for your thesis.


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06, L07, L08
learning_activities.topic_description: Professional Ethics I & Research Ethics We will discuss professional issues around establishing appropriate boundaries during psychology practice. We will discuss the importance of maintaining confidentiality. We will consider ethical issues related to conducting research. We will also consider different research methodologies appropriate for your masters thesis. We will start to workshop ideas for your thesis.

learning_activities.topic:
Professional Ethics II and Peer Supervision Case Presentations I

We consider the importance of record keeping. We will consider the use of AI in clinical practice.

We have a guest speaker, Prof Gene Moyle, who is Pro-Vice Chancellor from QUT, who will discuss ethical issues associated with working with athletes.

We will have the first group of Peer Supervision Case Presentations.


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06, L07, L08

learning_activities.topic:
Professional Ethics III and Peer Supervision Case Presentations II

In this workshop, we consider particular ethical issues when working with young people. We consider unique issues when working with First Nations populations.

We have a guest speaker, Marie Zitny, who is a Senior Manager at the Integrity Unit at UQ. She will discuss issues related to maintaining ethics and integrity in a large organization.

We will have the second group of Peer Supervision Case Presentations.


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L08

She will discuss issues related to maintaining ethics and integrity in a large organization. We will have the second group of Peer Supervision Case Presentations.

learning_activities.topic:
Professional Ethics IV & Research Presentations I

We will consider ethical issues associated with bullying and harassment in the workplace. We will consider issues associated with both making and receiving a complaint.

The second part of the workshop will involve students presenting their research proposal to the class.


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06, L07, L08
learning_activities.topic_description: Professional Ethics IV & Research Presentations I We will consider ethical issues associated with bullying and harassment in the workplace. We will consider issues associated with both making and receiving a complaint. The second part of the workshop will involve students presenting their research proposal to the class.

learning_activities.topic:
Professional Ethics V & Research Presentations II

In this workshop, we will discuss ethical issues associated with self-care. Depending on time and class interest, we may cover and additional current ethical issues. We will review the main themes from the course. We will spend some time preparing for the "Ethical Clinic" competency.

The second part of the workshop will involve students presenting their research proposal to the class.


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06, L07, L08
